title: Hypotheses, predictions, and discriminating comparisons

objectives.statement: Distinguish an empirical question, a hypothesis, and a prediction in a short scientific case.
Derive an observable prediction from a stated hypothesis and conditions, and name a result that would count against it.
Compare competing hypotheses by deriving outcomes on which they disagree and interpret the evidence without claiming proof or unsupported causation.

scenes.title: From what you want to know to what you expect to see
scenes.contentHtml:
From what you want to know to what you expect to see

Imagine that you have already asked this bounded question:

For freshly cut Granny Smith apple slices kept at 22–24 °C, what is the difference in time to reach browning level 3 between lemon-juice-coated and water-coated slices during the first 60 minutes?

If you cannot yet identify the observation, comparison, and conditions in that question, pause and complete the preceding lesson, From an observation to a testable question, before continuing.

The question tells you what you want to find out. It does not yet say why the coatings might differ or what record you expect.

If the data arrived today, how could you tell whether they challenge an explanation rather than merely decorate a story made afterward? You need two more links: a proposed answer and the observable result that answer commits you to.

Before reading on, label each sentence question, hypothesis, or prediction:

  1. A dissolved substance specific to lemon juice slows browning under these conditions.
  2. Will lemon-juice-coated and water-coated slices take different times to reach browning level 3?
  3. If the lemon-specific explanation is correct under the stated conditions, the lemon-coated slices will take longer to reach level 3 than the water-coated slices.

The order is hypothesis, question, prediction. The three sentences do different jobs.

Part of the reasoning Its job Useful form
Empirical question Names what evidence should help answer What difference or pattern is present?
Hypothesis Proposes an explanation or relationship The difference occurs because…
Prediction States the record expected if the hypothesis holds under named conditions If the hypothesis and conditions hold, then we expect to observe…

A hypothesis is not merely a topic or a guess. It is a proposed explanation or relationship that can meet evidence. A useful hypothesis must risk being wrong: ask what result would count against it.

A prediction is not the same as saying what you hope will happen. It is a logical bridge from the hypothesis to an observable record:

hypothesis + stated conditions → expected observation

The conditions matter. A hypothesis might lead to one expectation at room temperature and another outside that range. This lesson names conditions so you can see the reasoning; deciding how to control and measure them comes next.

Write the prediction before you inspect the relevant result. That keeps you from quietly changing the expected result to match what you have already seen. Some fields use prediction mainly for a novel or future observation. In this lesson, an archived but still unseen record can also test the logic, provided you lock the prediction before opening that record.

One question can have competing answers

Suppose a second student proposes:

Changing the coating from water to lemon juice does not change the browning time under these conditions.

That is a different hypothesis. It leads to a different expectation: lemon-coated and water-coated slices should reach level 3 at about the same time. Now the same record can separate two explanations because the explanations do not expect the same outcome.

The strongest comparison is not “Did my preferred result occur?” It is “Which result should occur under each plausible hypothesis, and where do their expectations differ?”

Check
id check-role-retrieval

Without looking back, complete the two blanks so the chain has three distinct parts:

What we want to find out → ___ → ___

Then add the observation that would count against your hypothesis.

A useful reconstruction is: empirical question → proposed explanation or relationship → expected observation under stated conditions. If you cannot name a contrary observation, make either the hypothesis, conditions, or record more specific.

Source Note
claims claim-role-distinctions, claim-conditional-prediction, claim-discriminating-comparison, claim-before-inspectionsources source-usgs-rigor, source-epa-causal-assessment

The USGS-hosted article abstract supports the distinction among a research question, plausible explanatory hypotheses, predictions derived from them, and comparison with data. The EPA causal-assessment page supports stating predictions before viewing the relevant observation as a guard against after-the-fact adjustment. The apple hypotheses and all wording, values, and tables here are original course examples.

scenes.title: Make two explanations disagree
scenes.contentHtml:
Make two explanations disagree

Return to the apple question. The case and numerical results below are fictional. You do not need apples, laboratory access, or personal experience with browning to follow the reasoning.

Worked Example
id worked-example-apple-comparison

1. Start with the question.

For freshly cut Granny Smith apple slices kept at 22–24 °C, what is the difference in time to reach browning level 3 between lemon-juice-coated and water-coated slices during the first 60 minutes?

2. State competing hypotheses.

  • Hypothesis A: A dissolved substance specific to lemon juice slows browning under these conditions.
  • Hypothesis B: Changing the coating from water to lemon juice does not change the browning time under these conditions.

These are proposals for the exercise, not established claims about apple chemistry.

3. Hold the relevant conditions in view.

Both hypotheses refer to freshly cut Granny Smith slices, the two stated coatings, 22–24 °C, and the first 60 minutes. If those conditions change, derive again instead of carrying the prediction over automatically.

4. Derive each expected record.

If this hypothesis holds under the stated conditions… …then this record is expected
A: a lemon-specific dissolved substance slows browning Lemon-coated slices take longer than water-coated slices to reach level 3
B: changing between these coatings does not change browning time Lemon-coated and water-coated slices reach level 3 at about the same time

The time difference is a discriminating prediction because the two hypotheses expect different results. Merely predicting “the slices will brown” would not separate them.

5. Compare with the supplied fictional result.

Suppose the summary record reports a median (the middle recorded time) of 41 minutes for the lemon-coated slices and 23 minutes for the water-coated slices.

That direction agrees with A's prediction and conflicts with B's equal-time prediction. In this comparison, the record therefore supports A over B.

6. Stop where the evidence stops.

The result does not prove that a particular lemon substance caused the difference. Other explanations may make the same prediction, and the result's strength depends on how samples, measurements, and conditions were handled. The careful conclusion is:

Under the stated fictional conditions, the result is consistent with Hypothesis A and counts against Hypothesis B as written. It does not by itself establish the mechanism or eliminate untested explanations.

Notice the decisions that make the comparison useful:

  • The hypotheses answer the same question.
  • Each prediction names the observation, not only a vague direction such as “it will work.”
  • Conditions appear before the arrow.
  • The expected records disagree.
  • The conclusion compares support without turning agreement into proof.

Now alter the fictional result: both groups reach level 3 at a median of 27 minutes. Before reading on, decide which hypothesis that result counts against and which it supports.

The equal-time result counts against A as written and agrees with B's prediction. It would not prove B. A third hypothesis might also expect equal times.

This is why contrary results are productive. They tell you which part of an explanation may need rejection, narrowing, or revision.

Source Note
claims claim-role-distinctions, claim-conditional-prediction, claim-discriminating-comparison, claim-before-inspection, claim-evidence-boundarysources source-usgs-rigor, source-epa-causal-assessment

The sources support deriving observable consequences from hypotheses, comparing them with evidence, reducing the set of plausible explanations, and treating agreement as evidence rather than automatic causal proof. The worked example, alternative hypotheses, values, reasoning table, and conclusions are independently created fictional material.

scenes.title: Practise the reasoning and repair shortcuts
scenes.contentHtml:
Practise the reasoning and repair shortcuts

Begin with retrieval. Without looking back, write one sentence for each job:

  • an empirical question;
  • a hypothesis; and
  • a prediction.

Then draw the arrow hypothesis + conditions → expected observation. If the sentence after the arrow explains why, it is probably another hypothesis rather than a prediction.

One part supplied

A fictional mystery box beeped when a red magnetic token was inserted.

  • Question: Which observable property of a token is associated with the beep?
  • Hypothesis: The box beeps when the inserted token is magnetic. Treat this as the complete explanation of when the box beeps.

Complete the prediction for a blue magnetic token and a red nonmagnetic token. Commit your answer before continuing.

One defensible prediction is: if magnetism triggers the beep under the same box settings, then the blue magnetic token will produce a beep and the red nonmagnetic token will not. Token color is changed independently of magnetism, so this expected pattern separates the proposed property from color.

A result that counts against the hypothesis as written would be a blue magnetic token that does not produce a beep while a red nonmagnetic token does under the same settings.

Make two hypotheses disagree

A fictional greenhouse alarm sounds unexpectedly. Two explanations are proposed:

For this simplified comparison, treat each hypothesis as the complete explanation of when the alarm sounds:

  • Hypothesis T: the alarm sounds when the sensor temperature is above its threshold and otherwise remains silent.
  • Hypothesis L: the alarm sounds when stray light reaches the sensor and otherwise remains silent.

Copy the table and fill every blank before reading the feedback.

Condition Prediction from Hypothesis T Prediction from Hypothesis L
Bright but below the temperature threshold ___ ___
Dark but above the temperature threshold ___ ___

The discriminating expectations are:

Condition Prediction from Hypothesis T Prediction from Hypothesis L
Bright but below the temperature threshold Alarm does not sound Alarm sounds
Dark but above the temperature threshold Alarm sounds Alarm does not sound

If you first thought both mechanisms could operate, that is sensible caution about a real device. This exercise instead tests each simplified hypothesis as the complete explanation, so return to that stated assumption and ask what each one commits you to under the two conditions.

Less support

A covered cup ended a classroom demonstration warmer than an uncovered cup. Treat the observation as fictional; do not perform the demonstration.

Write:

  1. one hypothesis about reduced energy transfer to the surroundings;
  2. a different hypothesis about the two cups having started at different temperatures;
  3. a prediction from each under one clearly named comparison; and
  4. the result on which the two predictions differ.

A defensible comparison would begin both cups at the same recorded temperature, use otherwise matched cups, and compare later temperatures. The reduced-transfer hypothesis predicts that the covered cup will end warmer. The different-starting-temperature hypothesis, as the complete explanation, predicts no consistent covered-versus-uncovered difference once the starting temperatures are matched. The later temperature difference is therefore discriminating for these two simplified hypotheses.

Your wording can differ. Check that each prediction follows from its own hypothesis and named conditions rather than from the answer you prefer.

Misconception
id misconception-matching-proves

Commit first. A prediction matches a result. Does that prove the hypothesis? Answer yes or no and give one reason.

Tempting shortcut: “My prediction was right, so my explanation is true.”

Why the shortcut fails: more than one explanation can lead to the same expected record. Measurement problems or unexamined conditions may also matter. A match supports the hypothesis in the comparison made; it does not automatically establish a cause.

Repair: name the competitors and use calibrated language: consistent with, supports relative to, or counts against. Then ask what different record the remaining hypotheses predict.

Recheck: Two hypotheses both predict that a sealed jar's mass will remain unchanged over ten minutes. The mass remains unchanged. What can you conclude? A careful answer says the result agrees with both predictions and therefore does not discriminate between those hypotheses.

Independent practice

Choose an ordinary observation that does not require you to collect new data. Write two plausible hypotheses, state the conditions, and derive one expected record from each. Highlight the place where the predictions differ. Finally, name a possible result that counts against each hypothesis.

If both hypotheses always predict the same thing, either revise the comparison or add a third condition that makes their commitments visible. Do not add complexity merely to sound scientific; add only what changes the expected observation.

Source Note
claims claim-role-distinctions, claim-conditional-prediction, claim-discriminating-comparison, claim-before-inspection, claim-evidence-boundarysources source-usgs-rigor, source-epa-causal-assessment

The sources support deriving predictions from hypotheses before inspecting the result, comparing expected observations with data, and using alternate explanations to calibrate inference. The mystery box, greenhouse alarm, cup case, jar recheck, and all expected outcomes are original fictional teaching examples.

scenes.title: Transfer the logic to an archived record
scenes.contentHtml:
Transfer the logic to an archived record

This final case uses a fictional archive. No frogs were observed and no recordings were collected for this lesson.

An audio team reports fewer detected frog calls in colder night-time intervals than in warmer intervals. Each audio file also contains an automatically inserted calibration tone that should be detectable when the recorder operates normally.

Two hypotheses are proposed:

  • Hypothesis 1: the apparent pattern reflects calling activity that differs with temperature, while the recorder operates normally.
  • Hypothesis 2: calling activity does not differ systematically with temperature; cold-related recorder failure produced the apparent pattern.

The archive has already been collected, but its detailed call and calibration records are hidden from you. Before imagining that the archive is opened, complete this table:

Hypothesis Expected call record Expected calibration-tone record A result that counts against it
Calling differs with temperature ___ ___ ___
Recorder failure explains the pattern ___ ___ ___

One defensible completion is:

Hypothesis Expected call record Expected calibration-tone record A result that counts against it
Calling differs with temperature Fewer calls in colder intervals Calibration tones remain detectable across colder and warmer intervals The call difference appears only where calibration tones are missing or weak
Recorder failure explains the pattern Fewer recorded calls when the recorder fails Calibration tones are missing or weak in the same intervals, whether or not temperature alone separates them Calibration tones remain detectable while the call pattern still follows temperature

The calibration record is useful because the hypotheses make different commitments about it. If both hypotheses predicted only “fewer calls on cold nights,” the call count alone would not discriminate.

Now suppose the fictional archive shows intact calibration tones in every interval and still shows fewer calls in colder intervals. That result counts against Hypothesis 2 as written and is consistent with Hypothesis 1. It does not prove that temperature caused the pattern. Other features may track temperature, and a later study would need suitable variables, controls, and measurements to support a stronger conclusion.

Synthesize the reasoning

Complete this chain from memory:

empirical question → ___ → conditions → ___ → compare with evidence → revise

A useful reconstruction is:

empirical question → hypothesis → conditions → prediction → compare with evidence → revise

For competing hypotheses, write one chain for each and look for a record where the predictions differ. That differing record makes a test discriminating.

Mastery check

The assessment contains three untimed, retakeable items worth ten points. Attempt each before opening its answer and reasoning. Mastery means earning all ten points: two for distinguishing the three roles, four for deriving a prediction and a contrary result, and four for constructing a discriminating comparison in a new two-record case. If you miss a criterion, repair the missing link in the reasoning chain and retry with the alternate prompt in the feedback.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every task can be completed in prose, bullets, or a table. You may type, dictate into your own device, or use assistive technology; no recording or upload is required. The lesson does not depend on color, images, sound, dragging, speed, physical apple preparation, animal contact, or laboratory access. The audio case is fully represented in text, including the role of the calibration tone.

Recovery route

If question, hypothesis, and prediction blur together, ask three verbs: ask, explain, expect. A question asks; a hypothesis explains or proposes a relationship; a prediction states what record to expect under named conditions.

If the arrow stalls, write the hypothesis after Suppose… and the conditions after under…. Then finish: If both hold, I expect to observe…

If two hypotheses will not separate, make a two-column table. Hold the observation conditions constant and fill in each expected record. Add a comparison only when it makes the columns differ.

If a matching result feels like proof, list one untested alternative and restate the conclusion using consistent with or counts against.

The previous lesson supplied bounded empirical questions. The next lesson will ask how variables and controls make these comparisons fair and observable. A later lesson will return to the limits of models and explanations. Here, the target is the logical bridge from a proposed answer to a discriminating expected record.

Source Note
claims claim-role-distinctions, claim-conditional-prediction, claim-discriminating-comparison, claim-before-inspection, claim-evidence-boundarysources source-usgs-rigor, source-epa-causal-assessment

The sources support the question–hypothesis–prediction chain, comparison of candidate explanations, use of predicted observations as evidence, and cautious conclusions when alternatives remain. The archived-audio case, calibration comparison, records, tables, and feedback are original fictional teaching material.

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable for this lesson. Material authoring assistance used Codex by OpenAI, model GPT-5, in the isolated run a1fab976-9564-4c22-882e-ef9ded502531 on 2026-07-20. The instructions digest covers the exact UTF-8 task payload supplied by the root agent, including its terminal newline; it excludes hidden provider and system instructions.

items.prompt: A student writes three statements about a fictional lamp: (1) 'Does the lamp's light output change as battery voltage falls?' (2) 'Lower voltage reduces the energy transferred to the lamp each second.' (3) 'If that explanation holds for this lamp, measured light output will be lower at 2.5 V than at 3.0 V.' Which option labels the statements correctly?

answer.summary: The first statement asks what happens, the second proposes why, and the third states an expected record under named conditions.
answer.reasoning: Statement 1 leaves an empirical result open. Statement 2 proposes an explanatory relationship. Statement 3 begins from that explanation and conditions to name the observable light-output comparison. If you reversed the last two, look for the sentence that names a measurement rather than a mechanism. On a retry, classify these three statements: 'Do wet and dry paper strips bend by different angles?', 'Water weakens bonds within the paper', and 'If that explanation holds, the wet strip will bend through a larger angle under the same load.'
acceptableResponses: The option identifying statement 1 as the question, statement 2 as the hypothesis, and statement 3 as the prediction.
commonErrors: Treating an explanatory mechanism as a prediction
Treating a question as if it were a true-or-false hypothesis

items.prompt: In a fictional sensor, the hypothesis is: 'The status light turns on when pressure on the pad exceeds 20 N.' Treat that hypothesis as the complete explanation of when the light turns on. The same calibrated sensor receives a 25 N load and then a 15 N load. State that fixed sensor condition, write the two-part observable prediction, and name one result that would count against the hypothesis as written.

answer.correct: A complete response predicts that the status light will turn on for the 25 N load and remain off for the 15 N load on the same calibrated sensor. It also names a conflicting result, such as the light remaining off at 25 N or turning on at 15 N under those conditions.
answer.summary: The prediction follows the stated threshold in both directions, and a contrary observation exposes what the hypothesis risks.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for preserving the same calibrated-sensor condition, one for predicting light on at 25 N, one for predicting light off at 15 N, and one for a specific observed result that conflicts with at least one of those expectations. All four are required for 4/4. If your answer only restates the hypothesis, replace the pressure relationship with the actual light records expected at the two loads. On a retry, use a 50 °C threshold and predict an indicator's state at 55 °C and 45 °C.
acceptableResponses: Any response that keeps the stated conditions, predicts on at 25 N and off at 15 N, and names an incompatible light result under the same conditions.
commonErrors: Repeating the explanatory relationship without naming an observable light state
Predicting only one of the two loads
Calling a matching result evidence against the hypothesis
Changing the sensor or calibration when stating the contrary result

items.prompt: A fictional museum entry logger has an archive of detections from afternoon and late-evening intervals, but its detailed entry counts and clock-comparison records are hidden. An independent building clock can be compared with the timestamp stored for each detection. Hypothesis 1 says actual entry activity is lower during late-evening intervals while the logger clock operates normally. Hypothesis 2 says entry activity does not differ systematically by time of day and a logger-clock error would create an apparent late-evening decrease. State one prediction from each hypothesis that uses both entry detections and the clock comparison, identify the discriminating result, and explain what agreeing timestamps plus fewer entries in late-evening intervals would and would not justify concluding.

answer.correct: A complete response predicts fewer late-evening entries with logger timestamps agreeing with the independent building clock for Hypothesis 1, and an apparent time-of-day pattern accompanied by logger timestamps that disagree with the building clock for Hypothesis 2. It identifies timestamp agreement or mismatch alongside the entry pattern as discriminating. Given agreeing timestamps and fewer late-evening entries, it concludes that the record is consistent with Hypothesis 1 and counts against Hypothesis 2 as written, while not claiming that time of day is proven causal or that all alternatives are eliminated.
answer.summary: The independent clock check makes the hypotheses disagree, and the conclusion is calibrated to that comparison.
answer.reasoning: Score one point for an entry-plus-agreeing-clock prediction from Hypothesis 1, one for an apparent-entry-pattern-plus-clock-mismatch prediction from Hypothesis 2, one for explicitly identifying clock agreement as the discriminating record, and one for the paired interpretation: supports H1 over H2 as written but does not prove causation or eliminate untested alternatives. All four are required for 4/4. If both predictions mention only entry counts, add the independent clock comparison. If the conclusion says 'proves,' name an untested alternative and recalibrate the verb. On a retry, compare a real drop in delivery scans with a scanner-clock error using both scan counts and synchronization records.
acceptableResponses: Any logically equivalent pair of predictions and cautious interpretation that uses agreement with the independent clock to distinguish an activity pattern from a timestamp-error explanation.
commonErrors: Giving identical entry-count-only predictions for both hypotheses
Failing to name the independent clock comparison that separates the hypotheses
Claiming that agreement proves a time-of-day effect
Concluding nothing at all even though one stated hypothesis is challenged

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when statement 1 is identified as the empirical question; award 0 otherwise.
Award 1 point only when statement 2 is identified as the hypothesis and statement 3 as the prediction; award 0 if either is reversed.

rubrics.title: Prediction derived from a threshold hypothesis

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when the response keeps the same calibrated sensor or an explicitly equivalent fixed condition.
Award 1 point only when the light is predicted to turn on at 25 N.
Award 1 point only when the light is predicted to remain off at 15 N.
Award 1 point only when a specific light result that conflicts with the prediction under the same conditions is named.
Award 1 point only when Hypothesis 1 predicts fewer late-evening entries together with logger timestamps that agree with the independent building clock.
Award 1 point only when Hypothesis 2 predicts the apparent entry pattern together with logger timestamps that disagree with the independent building clock.
Award 1 point only when clock agreement alongside entry detections is identified as the record on which the hypotheses differ.

Award 1 point only when agreeing timestamps and fewer late-evening entries are interpreted as supporting Hypothesis 1 over Hypothesis 2 as written without claiming proof of causation or elimination of every alternative.

sources.title: Increased scientific rigor will improve reliability of research and effectiveness of management
sources.publisher: Journal of Wildlife Management; metadata and abstract hosted by the U.S. Geological Survey
sources.locator: USGS publication page, abstract, especially the six-part question–hypothesis–prediction–data–evaluation–inference sequence and the statement on reducing plausible explanations
sources.license: Publisher article rights are not asserted. The USGS metadata and abstract were used only as facts; no source expression or full text is reused.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson independently expresses the logical roles of questions, hypotheses, predictions, evidence comparison, and inference. All cases, values, tables, sequences of prompts, assessment items, and learner-facing wording are original. The publisher page and article full text were not processed.

agentAccess.notes: Before substantive access, the USGS copyrights-and-credits route was inspected. It describes USGS-authored or produced information as generally public domain and showed no term prohibiting this limited agent reading. The public USGS publication metadata-and-abstract page was then read without login, bypass, bulk collection, DOI redirection, or access to publisher-hosted full text. This is a dated access observation, not a warranty about third-party article rights.

sources.title: About Causal Assessment
sources.version: CADDIS Volume 1 web guidance as accessed 2026-07-20
sources.locator: An Approach to Causal Inference: Comparison of Candidate Causes, Rejection, Synthesis of Evidence; Causal Concepts: Predictive Performance
sources.license: U.S. federal government website material, used only as a factual and methodological reference. No wording, quotation, figure, table, example, or incorporated third-party material is reused.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson independently synthesizes the page's methodological points about specifying predictions before relevant observations, comparing alternate candidate explanations, and not treating a single result as causal proof. The lesson does not reproduce the page's wording, historical quotations, environmental examples, framework, scoring system, figures, or tables.

agentAccess.notes: Before substantive access, the EPA disclaimer and copyright route was inspected. No term prohibiting this limited agent reading of the public page was found. The public HTTPS causal-assessment page was then read without login, bypass, download, or bulk collection. This is a dated observation and does not extend to separately credited third-party material.

claims.statement: An empirical question identifies what is to be found out, a scientific hypothesis proposes an explanatory answer or relationship, and a prediction states an observable expectation derived from that hypothesis.
claims.scope: An introductory functional distinction for empirical scientific reasoning, not a claim that every discipline uses the three terms identically.
claims.uncertainty: Researchers may use hypothesis for descriptive, explanatory, statistical, or model-based propositions; local conventions should be made explicit.

claims.statement: A prediction should follow from a stated hypothesis together with relevant conditions and should name an observation that can be compared with evidence.
claims.scope: Introductory hypothesis-to-prediction reasoning; probabilistic predictions, formal statistical hypotheses, and model-specific derivations require additional methods.
claims.uncertainty: The precision and form of a prediction depend on the underlying model and the quality of available measurements.

claims.statement: When competing hypotheses predict different observations under the same relevant conditions, the observed result can discriminate among those hypotheses more effectively than a result they all predict.
claims.scope: Logical comparison among stated candidate hypotheses; study design, measurement quality, effect size, uncertainty, and completeness of alternatives must be evaluated separately.
claims.uncertainty: A practically observed record may be ambiguous or noisy even when idealized predictions differ.

claims.statement: Specifying the expected observation before inspecting the relevant result limits opportunities to alter the prediction after seeing the evidence.
claims.scope: A transparency safeguard for the lesson's reasoning exercises; it does not imply that all valuable evidence must be newly generated or prospective.
claims.uncertainty: The methodological weight of novel prediction compared with previously existing evidence is debated and varies with study purpose and design.

claims.statement: Agreement between a result and a prediction can support a hypothesis relative to stated alternatives, but one matching result alone does not prove a causal explanation or eliminate untested alternatives.
claims.scope: Introductory interpretation of hypothesis comparisons, especially causal proposals; it is not a substitute for field-specific standards of evidence.
claims.uncertainty: The warranted degree of support depends on design quality, measurement, prior knowledge, plausible alternatives, and the total evidence.

terms.definition: A question for which observations or measurements can bear on the answer.
A proposed explanation or relationship from which observable expectations can be derived.
An observable result expected if a stated hypothesis holds under stated conditions.
A relevant feature of the case, setting, or comparison under which a prediction is expected to hold.
Two or more proposed explanations or relationships considered as possible answers to the same question.
An expected observation for which competing hypotheses give different outcomes, allowing evidence to count differently for them.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson content

Hypotheses, predictions, and discriminating comparisons, version 0.1.0, is original course content contributed by VSBDev with disclosed Codex assistance. It is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).

Suggested attribution: “Hypotheses, predictions, and discriminating comparisons” by VSBDev for EmbeddedKnowledge, version 0.1.0, CC BY 4.0.

Sources and reuse

The USGS publication metadata-and-abstract page listed as source-usgs-rigor was used only to verify the functional distinctions among a research question, hypotheses, predictions, evidence comparison, and inference. The publisher-hosted article was not processed. No article wording, example, figure, table, or other protected expression is reused or adapted.

The EPA page listed as source-epa-causal-assessment was used only to verify methodological points about specifying predictions before relevant observations, comparing candidate explanations, and keeping causal conclusions calibrated to the evidence. No EPA wording, environmental example, historical quotation, framework, scoring system, figure, table, or incorporated third-party material is reused or adapted.

The apple case continues an original fictional teaching case from the preceding EmbeddedKnowledge lesson. Its hypotheses, results, comparison table, and interpretation in this lesson are newly written. The mystery box, greenhouse alarm, covered cup, sealed jar, lamp, pressure sensor, frog-audio archive, and museum entry logger are also original fictional teaching cases. They do not report real studies, recordings, animals, learners, patients, or datasets.

No third-party assets or datasets are included.

Agent disclosure

VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is the accountable principal. Material authoring assistance used Codex by OpenAI, model GPT-5, version Codex agent runtime 2026-07-20, in isolated run a1fab976-9564-4c22-882e-ef9ded502531 on 2026-07-20. The recorded SHA-256 digest is sha256:a6085cd4cd1f4e412d2128d52fa454fad2834692479187b6ec31ec47e29d6e03; it covers the exact discloseable UTF-8 task payload supplied by the root agent, including its terminal newline, and excludes hidden provider and system instructions.
